coredata.dc:title: Spatially resolving and energy splitting of edge state in zigzag edged triangle graphene quantum dots on Cu(111) surface
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Sheng
Jin, Jing
Wang, Zhongping
Lu, Yan
Wang, Li
coredata.dc:description: The electronic structure of graphene nanoribbons (GNRs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) has been predicted to depend sensitively on the crystallographic orientation of their edges. However, direct observation of edge state for triangle graphene quantum dots (TGQDs) has not been verified experimentally. Here we explore, using the scanning tunneling spectroscopy (STS), the zigzag edged electronic property of varisized TGQDs. Predominantly zigzag-edged TGQDs exhibit edge-localized states with the energy splittings of about 0.2–0.3V when its lateral dimension is less than 7nm. The measured energy splittings agree with theoretical calculations, and show that these edge states originate from a hybridization effect of the substrate, and not from a magnetic splitting of the edge state.

L.W. acknowledges the Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University, Ministry of Education of China (NCET –11–1003) and Jiangxi Provincial “Ganpo Talented 555 Projects”.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characterization of atomically precise triangular TGQDs. (A) A 55×55nm2 STM topography of TGQDs on Cu (111) surface, which was recorded at a temperature of 77K (Ub=−2.0V, I=0.06nA), different dimensional islands are indicated by white dashed triangle. (B) Atomically resolved (14×14nm2) image of graphene Moiré pattern. (Ub=−0.3V, I=1.2nA). (C) Magnified view of Moiré pattern. (6×6nm2) (Ub=−0.3V, I=1nA).

Detection of zigzag-edge state for TGQD. (A) A 12×12nm2 low temperature STM image of TGQD on Cu(111) surface. (Ub=−0.50V, I=0.22nA). The 8nm edge length of TGQD is indicated. (B) (C) Individual dI/dV spectra measured at different points, as marked by the black and blue dots in panel (A), along the triangle diagonal line and the zigzag edge at T=77K. The dashed line in Fig. C is guides to the eyes. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Edge state of TGQD. (A) A 14×14nm2 low temperature STM image of TGQD with the critical edge length of 7nm. (Ub=−0.50V, I=0.20nA). (B) Differential conductance (dI/dV) spectra taken on various zigzag edge locations marked by black dots in Fig. A. The dash line shows the peak position.

Edge-state characterization of TGQD with 4.5nm edge length. (A) A two 14×14nm2 low temperature STM scan of TGQD with measured edge length is indicated. (Ub=−0.50V, I=0.20nA). (B) Individual dI/dV characteristics of the zigzag edge recorded on various locations (black dots as shown). (C) Experimentally estimated energy splitting value as a function of edge length of TGQDs. The solid line shows the inverse proportional fitting of the data when lateral dimension is less than 7nm. Error of the measured energy splitting values determined by thermal broadening and substrate effects. Error for graphene lateral dimension arises from tip convolution effects. (All the spectra have been shifted along the vertical axis for clarity).

Electronic structure of TGQD on Cu(111) surface. (A), (B) DOS calculated for the 2nm width TGQD without and with substrate. (C), (D) corresponding to the charge distributions of the two peaks labeled by yellow and green arrow in (B), respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Spatially resolving and energy splitting of edge state in zigzag edged triangle graphene quantum dots on Cu(111) surface

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanoscale Science and Technology Laboratory, Institute for Advanced Study, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanoscale Science and Technology Laboratory, Institute for Advanced Study, Nanchang University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The electronic structure of graphene nanoribbons (GNRs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) has been predicted to depend sensitively on the crystallographic orientation of their edges. However, direct observation of edge state for triangle graphene quantum dots (TGQDs) has not been verified experimentally. Here we explore, using the scanning tunneling spectroscopy (STS), the zigzag edged electronic property of varisized TGQDs. Predominantly zigzag-edged TGQDs exhibit edge-localized states with the energy splittings of about 0.2–0.3V when its lateral dimension is less than 7nm. The measured energy splittings agree with theoretical calculations, and show that these edge states originate from a hybridization effect of the substrate, and not from a magnetic splitting of the edge state.

ce:section.ce:para: Consisting of a single layer of carbon atoms arranged in a honeycomb lattice, graphene has been attracting much interest in recent years, due to its unique zero-gap electronic structure, which leads to electrons therein behave as massless Dirac fermion [1,2]. As a consequence of this, graphene exhibits a variety of exciting phenomena such as ballistic transport, anomalous integer quantum Hall effect and large coherence length, which all make graphene to be a promising material for novel thinner generation electronic components.

Compared to the armchair edge, nanostructures with zigzag edges are expected to host spin-polarized electronic edge states and can thus serve as key elements for graphene-based spintronics. A significant number of early theoretical studies have investigated specific zigzag-related one dimensional graphene nanoribbon revealing the presence of electronic states localized at the edges [3–7]. To introduce new properties, these graphene nanoribbons can be further functionalized by absorbing atoms such as H, O at their edges [8–11], the cluster like NH2, OH [12–14] and artificially generating isolated vacancies [15] or defects such as pentagon and heptagon pairs [16,17]. Even more intriguing are the properties of graphene nanohole [18] and quasi-zero dimensional nanodots [19–21] involving zigzag edges for which different levels of theory predict the existence of spin-polarized edge states.

Despite strong theoretical attention, direct experimental results related to edge states for zigzag edged graphene nanostructures remains extremely sparse and largely affected by very limited precise control over the edge orientation of the graphene [22–27]. Previous experimental observation of local electronic edge states of graphene has been achieved through the different technical means of measurement: The information about the nanographene edge state has been provided by means of polarization-dependent near edge x-ray absorption fine structure (NEXAFS) measurements [28]. The stability of the unique graphene edge states to surface adatom functionalization has been examined by electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS) [29]. A new type of edge state that has never been observed nor predicted before was showed on both the zigzag and bearded edges in photonic graphene lattices [30].

Recently, a systematic experimental verification of this fundamental prediction by means of STM/STS measurements was just only focused on graphene nanoribbons (GNRs) produced by various technologies: The local edges electronic structure has been characterized in defective edge of CVD grown nanoribbons [31]; Measurements in GNRs synthesized by unzipping of carbon nanotubes have indeed demonstrated the presence of GNRs edge magnetism, however, these GNRs are usually terminated by chiral edges [32]; The recent claims of room temperature edge ferromagnetism in GNRs tailored by lithography are also based on STM spectroscopy [33]; The latest advances in GNRs with precise zigzag edges through surface-assisted chemical synthesis has exhibited the existence of edge-localized states with large energy splitting [34].

For graphene quantum dots, the observation of localized edge state has concentrated on irregular topography with less-well-defined zigzag edges [35,36]. Whereas, the edge electronic structure has never been detected on TGQDs, here, the investigated TGQDs with zigzag edges periphery on Cu(111) surface were synthesize by bottom-up fabrication, which could lead to new methods for experimentally verifying the electronic, optical and magnetic properties predicted by theory [37–39]. By probing the local electronic structure of TGQDs using the resolved scanning tunneling spectroscopy data, we observed the non-magnetism edge states of TGQDs. Interestingly, an energy splitting of 0.2–0.3V of the edge state is produced when TGQDs edge length is small than 7nm, which is in accordance with applying density functional theory (DFT) calculations and reflects the dominant role of a hybridization of edge state with the Cu substrate.

Dependent on the size and dimensionality, finite-sized graphene has attracted attention for its particular electronic structure. Our TGQDs were prepared in a same way as we did before [40], the bottom-up synthesis of triangle graphene dots ( Fig. 1A) on single crystal Cu(111) was achieved by thermal dissociating of 1,10-ferrocenedicarboxylic acid molecules at temperatures of 525K for 15min in an ultrahigh vacuum chamber, the majority of triangle-shape islands has edges that terminates at Cu(111) step edges and appears to affect their shape. Determined by scanning tunneling microscopy, the image of these epitaxial grown nanostructures shows that graphene islands have different sizes which can be precisely controlled depending on growth parameters, although about 10nm edge lengths of the islands is the most common from the statistic data over hundreds of islands. The STM image measuring on the central part of the triangle graphene islands shows a clear superstructure as shown in Fig. 1B. And this pattern is known as moiré structure, which is a result of overlaying the hexagonal lattice of the graphene with the lattice of the Cu(111). The moiré pattern identifies the formation of an epitaxial ordered graphene layer. Looking more closely at the topography of graphene on Cu(111), atomic-resolution imaging of the graphene (Fig. 1C) is easily accessible at higher tunneling currents, the basic vectors of this superstructure is measured to be A=B=1.00±0.03nm as same as our previous experiment. The obtained high resolution edge image confirmed the carbon atoms on the edge of this triangle graphene are arranged in zigzag form [40].

The synthesis of TGQDs with zigzag edge periphery enables us to investigate experimentally its local electronic structure using STS, which are detected with various bias voltage and reflects the energy dependence of local density of states (LDOS). A typical constant current STM scan of graphene epitaxially grown on Cu surface is shown in Fig. 2A, and it is estimated that the maximum edge length for this triangle graphene is about 8nm. Figs. 2B, 2C shows the dI/dV spectra obtained at different positions along diagonal line and near the zigzag edge of triangle graphene, as pictured in Fig. 2A, respectively. The STS measured at interior region shows a nearly linear relationship with the applied bias voltage, as shown in Fig. 2B. The curves measured from triangle vertex region and internal locations for triangle graphene make no significant difference.

On the contrary, when positioning the STM tip above a zigzag edge vicinity of the triangle graphene, the differential conductance (dI/dV) spectrum, as shown in Fig. 2C, exhibits an additional broad slight peak (indicated by dashed line) centered about at approximately −0.12V below the Fermi level (EF) except for curves measured at triangle vertex region. The STS discrepancy between Figs. 2B and 2C confirms that the peak observed near the zigzag edge region is a localized state derived from the termination of π bonding, which is identified as edge state [32].

We have also measured tunneling spectra for TGQDs with edge lengths of about 7nm, as shown in Fig. 3. The individual STS spectrums of this graphene are plotted in Fig. 3B, which are collected at various sites (as marked in Fig. 3A) along the edge. In this situation, a clear significantly localized state (indicated by dashed line) at about −0.2V at occupied state can be observed in STS measurements, which shows that the localized edge state is more noticeable in small size of TGQDs. The amplitude of the STS curves measured at center of the edge is larger than at vertex region, which is similar to the Fig. 2C, and also the amplitude of the localized state at −0.2V is maximized at the zigzag edge and spatially decays into the interior of the TGQDs (see Fig. S1). In addition, we have also probe the STS for TGQDs with variable sizes (see Fig. S2), and all of them shown similar edge-localized states. Thus, the localized edge state is an intrinsically property of TGQDs.

When the size of TGQDs is further decreased, we have observed additional feature in the STS spectra. Interestingly, the most dominant feature is two peaks rise up in each STS curve at the occupied state, measured at the zigzag edge, when boundary length is less than 7nm. For a TGQDs with about 4.5nm edge length is shown in Fig. 4A. In Fig. 4B, the dI/dV spectrums acquired along one of the edge of this TGQDs (as marked in Fig. 4A) clearly exhibits two resonance peaks (indicated by dashed line): one is located at about −0.4V and another is located at about −0.15V at the occupied states, which yielding a energy splitting of about 0.25V between the two occupied states. The peak positions of the two peaks are nearly not changed along the edge. For another graphene dots with irregular topography of about 6.3nm edge length, spectrum clearly exhibits the energy splitting of about 0.21V shown in Supplementary Fig. S3. The relationship between the energy splitting and the size of TGQDs is further determined, as summarized in Fig. 4C. There is a sudden changes at about 7nm in this relationship curve: energy splitting vanishes when lateral length of TGQDs is greater than 7nm; however, when the lateral length of TGQDs is less than about 7nm, an energy splitting of about 0.2–0.3V can be detected, and slightly increase as the size of TGQDs decrease.

An important issue concerned with the zigzag edged TGQDs is the energy splitting of edges state. In a previous article of ZGNRs, similar sudden changes of energy splitting at about 7nm width is also observed, and it was explained by the antiferromagnetic-ferromagnetic phase transition at this ribbons width [41]. In order to show more clearly of the energy splitting in our experiments, the density of states (DOS) of a TGQD with about 2nm width was calculated by DFT. If the substrate is removed, there is a large DOS peak at the fermi energy in the absence of spin-polarization, as shown by the black DOS curve in Fig. 5A. However, this large DOS peak will split into two peaks on the two side of fermi energy in the presence of spin-polarization, and the split energy is about 0.71eV for a TGQD with about 2nm widths. Spin resolved DOS calculation shows that this two peaks corresponding to the two opposite spins. Although these calculation results are similar to our experimental results, there are two points that not meet with each other: One is that the calculated two peaks are not all below the fermi energy, and the other is that the calculated energy difference between the two peaks is larger than that of experimental.

Due to this two unconformities, we perform another calculation by taking the Cu(111) substrate into account. The calculated DOS are shown in Fig. 5B, which shows that the total DOS are also have two peaks (label by yellow and green arrow) slightly below the fermi energy: one is at about −0.35eV and another is at about −0.62eV, which result in an energy difference of about 0.27eV between the two peaks, which successful consistent with our experimental results. An important question is that whether the two peaks are also corresponding to the two opposite spins. In order to investigate this, the spin-resolved DOS are calculated and also shown in Fig. 5B, and it shows that the two peaks are not corresponding to the two opposite spins, instead, each spin have similar two peaks. This results are different to these calculated without Cu(111) substrate, which means that this two peaks observed in our experiment are formed by hybridization between TGQDs and Cu(111) substrate. From the STM simulations in Figs. 5C and 5D, it can be seen that the peak more near the fermi energy is more localized at edge sites, this is why we can detect the peaks at edges.

The local electronic DOS of TGQDs on Cu(111) surface was examined by STS that allows us to explore how electronic structure depends on the size of zigzag edged TGQDs. Our STS measurements reveal the presence of non-magnetism localized edge states, the amplitude of which is spatially decays into the interior of the TGQDs. Furthermore, for smaller size TGQDs (lateral dimension is small than 7nm), the non-magnetic energy splitting of about 0.2–0.3V at edge state are slightly increase with the decreased TGQDs size. The behavior of TGQDs matches our theoretical calculation by applying DFT, indicating no magnetic edge state exits in TGQDs placed on Cu(111) surface due to the hybridization effect of the substrate. Our successful bottom-up synthesis of atomically TGQDs opens extensive opportunities and challenges for the analysis of their physical properties (band structure, magnetism and charge transport) and for the fabrication of graphene-based devices.

The experiments were carried out under ultra-high vacuum conditions (base pressure: 8×10−11 mbar) with a low-temperature STM from Createc. A copper single crystal (111) (Kejin Mater Co., China) was used as the substrate for the growth of TGQDs. The surface was cleaned by repeated cycles of Ar+ bombardment (3keV) and annealing (600K) for 15min until a clean surface was obtained, as judged by STM. After several hours of degassing, 1,10-ferrocenedicarboxylic acid molecules (purity 99%) was thermally evaporated from a Knudsen cell at 400K onto a pre-clean Cu surface held at room temperature. After deposition, the sample was post-annealed directly to 525K for 15min to achieve high-quality TGQDs.

All the STM/STS measurements were obtained with a chemically etched tungsten tip. STM images were acquired in the constant-current mode at sample temperatures of 77K, as indicated in each case. Scanning parameters are specified in each figure caption. The scanning tunneling spectroscopy measurements were operated by using the lock-in detection, with a bias voltage modulation of frequency 467Hz and amplitude 50mVpp for the dI/dV spectroscopic. To ensure that our STM tips were free of anomalies in electronic structure, the STM tips used were electronically calibrated on clean Cu(100) or Cu(111) surface for local dI/dV spectroscopy before and after measurement of a graphene/Cu sample (Supplementary Fig. S5).

Density functional theory (DFT) as implemented in the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) [42,43] was applied using the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) generalized-gradient exchange and correlation functional [44]. A plane-wave basis set with a kinetic energy cutoff of 400eV is used. The van der Waals interaction was considered with the optB86b-vdW method [45]. Four layers of Cu atoms, separated by a 18Å vacuum region, were employed to model the Cu (111) surface. A 1×1×1 k-mesh was adopted for geometry optimization and STM simulations. In geometry optimizations, all atoms expect those for the bottom two Cu layers were fully relaxed until the residual force per atom was less than 0.02eV/Å. The STM simulation was performed using the Tersoff-Hamann approximation.

L.W. conceived and designed the experiments. S.W. performed the experiments. J.J. assisted the experiments. Y.L. carried out simulated calculations. All authors discussed the results and analyzed the data and commented on the manuscript.
The authors declare that they have no competing financial interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants 61474059, 11504158, and U1432129), National Key Basic Research Program of China (2013CB934200). L.W. acknowledges the Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University, Ministry of Education of China (NCET –11–1003) and Jiangxi Provincial “Ganpo Talented 555 Projects”.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.physe.2017.01.024.
